ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for 'prawn rice bowl'; FOODON_00002809 (prawn dish) used as closest defensible parent anchor. Specific bowl/rice combination not represented in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans and products thereof — cooked prawn/shrimp dish with rice
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest defensible parent match for cooked crustacean dish. No exact code exists for Malaysian-style prawn rice bowl in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish type; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one bowl (approximately 400 g), as listed on the GrabFood menu for EC Coffee Bar, comprising rice, prawns, vegetables, and broth or sauce.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food (Prawn dishes and rice-based mixed dishes)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Deep Dive Prawn Bowl' exists in the HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing HPB data for cooked prawns (stir-fried/sautéed), steamed white rice, and mixed vegetable broth in proportions consistent with a standard Malaysian-style prawn rice bowl (approximately 30% rice, 35% prawns, 20% broth/sauce, 15% vegetables). Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this composite and retained where consistent. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked shrimp (NDB 15152) and cooked white rice (NDB 20045) used as secondary cross-reference. Values are marked medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — White rice (boiled) analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Deep Dive Prawn Bowl'. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being white rice (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The prawn, vegetable, and broth components contribute negligible carbohydrate and are expected to moderately attenuate the glycaemic response through protein and fibre co-ingestion, supporting a GI estimate at the lower end of the white rice range (65). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 45.0) / 100 = 29.3 (carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 11.25 × 4 = 45.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 300 mg/100 g (1200 mg per 400 g bowl) with limited potassium offset (~180 mg/100 g estimated)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt broth and sauce on the side, as a full bowl provides approximately 1200 mg sodium — more than half the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — with insufficient potassium to offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (45 g per bowl) from white rice with a medium GI of 65 and glycemic load of 29.3 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request brown rice or a reduced rice portion to lower the glycaemic load, as the 400 g bowl provides approximately 45 g of carbohydrate primarily from white rice (GI ~65), while the substantial protein content (35 g per bowl) from prawns helps moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (300 mg/100 g) and moderate cholesterol (62.5 mg/100 g) from prawns, partially offset by low saturated fat (1.5 g/100 g) and anti-inflammatory aromatics
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and request reduced-salt preparation, as the high sodium content (1200 mg per bowl) is the primary cardiovascular concern, while the low saturated fat (6 g per bowl) and presence of anti-inflammatory ginger and garlic are favourable factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory aromatics (ginger, garlic) and low saturated fat, with caution for nightshade vegetables (tomato, chilli)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may generally consume this dish, as ginger and garlic provide anti-inflammatory benefit and saturated fat is low, but those with nightshade sensitivity should request reduced chilli and tomato as solanine may aggravate symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep Dive Prawn Bowl is a Malaysian-influenced café-style rice bowl dish served at EC Coffee Bar in Singapore. The dish features sautéed or pan-fried prawns as the primary protein, served over steamed white rice in a bowl with a flavourful broth or sambal-based sauce, aromatics including garlic and ginger, and green vegetables such as bok choy. The cooking method combines simmering for the broth and pan-frying or sautéing for the prawns and aromatics, resulting in a dish with tender, slightly charred prawns in a savoury, mildly spiced sauce. The texture profile is varied: soft rice, firm-tender prawns, and crunchy vegetables. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 120 kcal, 8.75 g protein, 4.5 g fat (1.5 g saturated), and 11.25 g carbohydrate, with 300 mg sodium — placing it in the moderate-to-high sodium category. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) based on the white rice component, with a glycaemic load of approximately 29 per 400 g bowl. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients, while the moderate carbohydrate load and medium GI require portion management for Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free. It is categorised under Malaysian cuisine and is typical of the fusion café dining culture in Singapore, where traditional Southeast Asian flavours are adapted for a café setting.
common_user_queries: Deep Dive Prawn Bowl EC Coffee Bar
Is prawn bowl good for diabetes?
Is prawn bowl high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'Deep Dive Prawn Bowl' exists in any approved primary nutrient database. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing HPB Singapore Foodo data for cooked prawns, steamed white rice, and mixed vegetable broth, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. The input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7 and provenance from GrabFood (a forbidden primary source); values were retained only where consistent with the approved-source composite. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a composite of HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked prawns, steamed white rice, and mixed vegetable broth; no exact approved-source entry exists for this dish. Input values (ai_generated, GrabFood provenance) were cross-checked and retained where consistent with the approved-source composite. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.2) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9) are estimated from the USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profile for cooked shrimp and rice; these are approximate and not directly reported in HPB Foodo for this dish type. 3. potassium_mg (180.0) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked shrimp and rice; not directly reported in HPB Foodo for this composite dish. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific Malaysian-style prawn rice bowl composite are not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central at the composite dish level as of 2024. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 65) based on white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). 6. The input ethnic_tags included 'Coffee' and 'Cake' which are artefacts of the GrabFood café category listing and have been excluded from the output as they do not reflect the dish's culinary identity. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the dish composition (prawns, rice, vegetables, aromatics); no formal halal certification has been verified. 8. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered. 9. Ontology match_type values corrected from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' for both foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type to conform to the allowed enumeration ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by g5_ontology_honesty.
